title_de: Die bewegende Geschichte hinter Don McLeans ikonischer Ballade 'Mountains o' Mourne'
title_nl: Ontdek de Magie van de Bergen: Het Hartverwarmende Verhaal Achter Don McLean's Iconische Ballade 'Mountains o' Mourne'
content_de:
Mountains o' Mourne – Irlands berühmtester Wanderberg und das Lied, das ihn unsterblich machte

Was ist „Mountains o' Mourne"?

Es gibt Landschaften, die sich ins Gedächtnis brennen. Die Mountains of Mourne in County Down, Nordirland, gehören dazu. Steil und dunkel steigen die Gipfel aus der Irischen See auf – eine Kulisse, die Dichter, Maler und Wanderer seit Jahrhunderten in ihren Bann zieht.

Das bekannteste Zeugnis dieser Faszination ist das Lied „Mountains of Mourne". Don McLean hat es 1971 auf seinem Album American Pie aufgenommen und damit einer irischen Volksmelodie Weltruhm beschert. Wer die Mournes einmal gesehen hat, versteht sofort, warum dieses Lied bis heute Menschen berührt.

Wer hat „Mountains O' Mourne" geschrieben?

Das Lied stammt von Percy French – Dichter, Komponist, Maler und wohl einer der charmantesten Entertainer, den Irland je hervorgebracht hat. French wurde am 1. Mai 1854 in Roscommon geboren und starb am 24. Juli 1920. Er war bekannt für seinen feinen Humor, seine Beobachtungsgabe und seine Fähigkeit, das Alltagsleben der irischen Landbevölkerung in eingängige Verse zu fassen.

Zu seinen bekanntesten Werken zählen neben „Mountains of Mourne" auch „Abdul Abulbul Amir" und „Are Ye Right, There Michael?". Der genaue Entstehungszeitpunkt von „Mountains of Mourne" ist nicht überliefert, doch Musikwissenschaftler datieren es auf das späte 19. Jahrhundert.

Wo liegen die Mountains of Mourne?

Die Mountains of Mourne liegen in County Down, Nordirland, und erstrecken sich über rund 570 Quadratkilometer. Das Massiv umfasst zwölf Gipfel über 600 Meter – darunter Slieve Donard mit 849 Metern, dem höchsten Berg Nordirlands.

Die Mournes fallen steil zur Küste der Irischen See ab. Dieses Zusammenspiel aus rauem Gebirge und glänzendem Wasser ist es, das Percy French mit den Worten „where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea" so treffend beschrieben hat.

Für Wanderbegeisterte aus Deutschland bieten die Mournes Touren für alle Schwierigkeitsgrade – von ruhigen Talwegen entlang des Silent Valley Reservoirs bis hin zu anspruchsvollen Gipfeltouren auf den Slieve Donard.

Eine bewegende Hommage

Don McLeans Interpretation von „Mountains o' Mourne" ist mehr als eine Coverversion. Es ist eine persönliche Liebeserklärung an Irland – gesungen von einem Iren in London, der in der Fremde arbeitet und von seiner Heimat träumt.

Die Melodie trägt eine melancholische Qualität in sich, die man im Englischen kaum übersetzen kann. Sie klingt nach nassem Gras, nach Nebel über den Gipfeln, nach dem Geruch von Torf – kurz: nach Irland.

McLean singt von seiner Sehnsucht nach den Mournes und von der Gewissheit, dass die Berge unverändert auf ihn warten. Der Refrain – „where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea" – zieht sich durch das gesamte Lied wie ein Anker, der den Erzähler mit seiner Heimat verbindet, egal wie weit er sich von ihr entfernt.

Ein Lied über Irland, die Menschen und die Natur

„Mountains o' Mourne“ ist auch ein Gesellschaftsporträt Percy French lässt seinen Erzähler aus dem London des späten 19. Jahrhunderts berichten – über die Baustellen, die fremden Gesichter, die merkwürdigen Sitten der Stadtbewohner. Der Humor ist zart und selbstironisch. Die Botschaft ist klar: Keine Stadt der Welt kann ersetzen, was man zu Hause zurückgelassen hat.

Für Wanderer, die die Mournes zu Fuß erkunden, wird diese Botschaft greifbar. Man spürt es in der Stille der Berge, im Wind über dem Mourne Wall, im Blick zurück auf die Küste von Carlingford Lough.

Die Liedtexte von „Mountains o' Mourne" – Original und Übersetzung

Hinweis: Die Liedtexte von Percy French / Don McLean sind urheberrechtlich geschützt. Die folgende Übersetzung dient dem Verständnis und ist keine offizielle Version.

Original (Englisch)

Übersetzung (Deutsch)

Oh, Mary, this London's a wonderful sight / With people here working by day and by night

Oh Mary, dieses London ist ein prächtiger Anblick / Hier arbeiten die Menschen bei Tag und bei Nacht

They don't sow potatoes nor barley nor wheat / But there's gangs of them diggin' for gold in the street

Sie pflanzen weder Kartoffeln noch Gerste noch Weizen / Doch Scharen von ihnen graben nach Gold in den Straßen

At least when I asked them, that's what I was told / So I just took a hand at this diggin' for gold

Zumindest sagten sie das, als ich fragte / Also versuchte ich mein Glück beim Goldgraben

But for all that I've found there, I might as well be / In the place where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea

Doch bei allem, was ich dort fand, könnte ich genauso gut sein / An dem Ort, wo die dunkle Mourne zum Meer hinabzieht

I believe that when writin' a wish you expressed / As to how the fine ladies of London were dressed

Ich glaube, du hast in einem Brief gefragt / Wie sich die feinen Damen Londons kleiden

But if you'll believe me, when asked to a ball / They don't wear no tops to their dresses at all

Doch wenn du mir glaubst: Wenn sie zum Ball eingeladen sind / Tragen sie gar keine Oberteile zu ihren Kleidern

Oh, I've seen them myself and you could not in truth / Tell if they were bound for a ball or a bath

Oh, ich habe sie selbst gesehen und du konntest wahrhaftig nicht sagen / Ob sie zum Ball gingen oder ins Bad

Don't be startin' them fashions now, Mary Mochree / In the place where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea

Fang bloß nicht mit diesen Moden an, Mary Mochree / An dem Ort, wo die dunkle Mourne zum Meer hinabzieht

There's beautiful girls here, oh, never you mind / Beautiful shapes Nature never designed

Es gibt wunderschöne Mädchen hier, oh, kümmere dich nicht darum / Schöne Formen, die die Natur nie entworfen hat

Lovely complexions of roses and cream / But let me remark with regard to the same

Liebliche Teints wie Rosen und Sahne / Doch lass mich dazu bemerken

That if at those roses you venture to sit / The colours might all come away on your lip

Dass wenn du dich diesen Rosen annäherst / Die Farben möglicherweise an deinen Lippen hängen bleiben

So I'll wait for the wild rose that's waitin' for me / In the place where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea

Daher warte ich auf die wilde Rose, die auf mich wartet / An dem Ort, wo die dunkle Mourne zum Meer hinabzieht

You remember young Diddy McClaren, of course / But he's over here with the rest of the force

Du erinnerst dich sicher an den jungen Diddy McClaren / Er ist hier drüben mit dem Rest der Polizei

I saw him one day as he stood on the strand / Stopped all the traffic with a wave of his hand

Ich sah ihn eines Tages, als er auf dem Strandweg stand / Mit einer Handbewegung hielt er den gesamten Verkehr an

As we were talking of days that are gone / The whole town of London stood there to look on

Während wir über vergangene Zeiten redeten / Stand ganz London da und schaute zu

But for all his great powers, he's wishful like me / To be back where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea

Aber bei all seiner Macht sehnt er sich wie ich / Zurück dorthin, wo die dunkle Mourne zum Meer hinabzieht

Wandern in den Mournes – was Sie erwartet

Die Mountains of Mourne bieten einige der eindrucksvollsten Wanderwege in Nordirland. Der Mourne Wall Trail (ca. 35 km) folgt der historischen Trockensteinmauer, die im frühen 20. Jahrhundert gebaut wurde und neun Gipfel miteinander verbindet. Der Aufstieg auf Slieve Donard (ca. 14 km, 850 Hm) ist der klassische Tagestrip – anspruchsvoll, aber lohnend.

Für Wanderer, die die Mournes als Teil einer längeren Irland-Reise erkunden möchten, bietet sich unsere Cooley & Mourne Wandertour an. Sie verbindet die Halbinsel Cooley im Süden mit den Mournes im Norden – zwei Landschaften, die unterschiedlicher kaum sein könnten und doch zusammengehören wie Meer und Berg.

Praktische Informationen für Wanderer aus Deutschland

Detail

Information

Lage

County Down, Nordirland

Höchster Punkt

Slieve Donard, 849 m

Beste Reisezeit

Mai bis September

Schwierigkeitsgrad

Mittel bis anspruchsvoll

Gepäcktransport

Ja, bei geführten/selbstgeführten Touren

GPS-Navigation

Offline-App verfügbar (WHI exklusiv)

Häufig gestellte Fragen

Wie schwierig sind die Wanderwege in den Mountains of Mourne? Das hängt von der gewählten Route ab. Familienfreundliche Wege entlang von Flüssen und Stauseen sind auch für weniger geübte Wanderer geeignet. Gipfeltouren wie der Slieve Donard erfordern gute Kondition und solides Schuhwerk.

Kann man die Mournes das ganze Jahr über wandern? Ja – aber der irische Winter bringt kurze Tage, nasses Wetter und mitunter eisige Gipfel. Am besten eignen sich Mai bis September für mehrtägige Wanderungen.

Sind die Mountains of Mourne Teil eines Nationalparks? Die Mournes liegen im Mourne Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty (AONB). Ein offizieller Nationalpark ist geplant, aber noch nicht ausgerufen.

Wie komme ich als Wanderer aus Deutschland in die Mournes? Flüge nach Belfast (BFS oder BHD) sind die schnellste Verbindung. Von dort sind die Mournes mit dem Mietwagen oder Bus in etwa einer Stunde erreichbar.

Bereit für Ihre Wanderreise nach Irland?

Die Mountains of Mourne sind mehr als eine Wanderkulisse. Sie sind ein Ort, der sich ins Gedächtnis einschreibt – so wie Percy French es vor über hundert Jahren in Verse gefasst hat und Don McLean es 1971 in eine Melodie goss.

Wenn Sie die Mournes selbst erleben möchten, helfe ich Ihnen gerne dabei. Ich bin Cliff von Walking Holiday Ireland und begleite Wanderer persönlich bei der Planung ihrer Irland-Reise – von der Routenwahl bis zum letzten Übernachtungsort.

Das könnte Sie auch interessieren.

  • Die besten Wanderwege in Nordirland

  • Antrim-Küste und Causeway Coast

  • Irland – Wann ist die beste Reisezeit?

  • Hikes in Killarney National Park
content_nl:
Waar gaat 'Mountains o' Mourne' over?

"Mountains o' Mourne", gezongen door Don McLean, is een klassieke ballade die het verhaal vertelt van een man die terugdenkt aan zijn jeugdherinneringen aan de prachtige Mountains of Mourne in Ierland. Het nummer werd in 1971 voor het eerst uitgebracht door Don McLean op het album "American Pie" en is sindsdien een vaste waarde in McLeans discografie geworden.

  • Waar gaat 'Mountains o' Mourne' over?

  • Wie schreef Mountains O' Mourne?

  • Waar liggen de Mountains of Mourne?

  • Een ontroerend eerbetoon

  • Een viering van het Ierse landschap en de verbinding met menselijkheid en liefde

  • Tekst van "Mountains o' Mourne" door Don McLean

'Mountains o' Mourne'

Wie schreef Mountains O' Mourne?

Het nummer "Mountains of Mourne" werd geschreven door Percy French. Percy French werd geboren in Roscommon, Ierland en was een songwriter, componist en schilder uit de late 19e en vroege 20e eeuw. Hij stond vooral bekend om zijn humoristische en satirische liedjes en was een populaire entertainer in Ierland en Groot-Brittannië.

Wanneer schreef Percy French de ballade 'Mountains O' Mourne'?

De exacte datum waarop Percy French het nummer "Mountains of Mourne" schreef is onbekend, maar men gelooft dat het in de late 19e eeuw werd geschreven. Enkele van zijn andere bekende werken zijn: "Abdul Abulbul Amir" en "Are Ye Right, There Michael." Hij werd geboren op 1 mei 1854 en overleed op 24 juli 1920.

Waar liggen de Mountains of Mourne?

De Mountains of Mourne, gelegen in County Down, Noord-Ierland, zijn een bergketen van ruige toppen die dramatisch oprijzen uit de zee. Met hun ruige schoonheid en natuurlijke pracht hebben de Mountains veel kunstenaars en dichters geïnspireerd en zijn ze het onderwerp geweest van talloze liedjes en ballades.

Een ontroerend eerbetoon

Don McLeans uitvoering van "Mountains o' Mourne" is een eerbetoon aan het gebied en een ontroerend eerbetoon aan de kracht en schoonheid van het Ierse landschap. Het nummer wordt gekenmerkt door zijn haunting melodie en evocatieve teksten, die de luisteraar transporteren naar een wereld van mistige heuvels, glooiende groene velden en glinsterende blauwe zeeën.

McLean zingt over zijn liefde voor de Mountains of Mourne en zijn verlangen om terug te keren naar deze bijzondere plek. Het refrein van het nummer is bijzonder memorabel, met zijn herhaling van "Oh, the Mountains of Mourne, they are standing tall and free, they call to me, they call to me." Dit eenvoudige maar krachtige refrein kan sterke emoties oproepen bij luisteraars en hen herinneren aan hun herinneringen en verbindingen met de plaatsen waar ze van houden.

Een viering van het Ierse landschap en de verbinding met menselijkheid en liefde

"Mountains o' Mourne" van Don McLean is een tijdloos eerbetoon aan de schoonheid van het Ierse landschap en een viering van de menselijke verbinding met de natuurlijke wereld. Met zijn haunting melodie en evocatieve teksten blijft dit nummer het publiek boeien en zal het ongetwijfeld voor vele generaties een klassieker blijven.

https://youtu.be/I4M_0Bvk10Q

Tekst van "Mountains o' Mourne" door Don McLean

Oh, Mary, this London's a wonderful sight
With people here working by day and by night
They don't sow potatoes nor barley nor wheat
But there's gangs of them diggin' for gold in the street
At least when I asked them, that's what I was told
So I just took a hand at this diggin' for gold
But for all that I've found there, I might as well be
In the place where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea

I believe that when writin' a wish you expressed
As to how the fine ladies of London were dressed
But if you'll believe me, when asked to a ball
They don't wear no tops to their dresses at all
Oh, I've seen them myself and you could not in truth
Tell if they were bound for a ball or a bath
Don't be startin' them fashions now, Mary Mochree,
In the place where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea

There's beautiful girls here, oh, never you mind
Beautiful shapes Nature never designed
Lovely complexions of roses and cream
But let me remark with regard to the same
That if at those roses you venture to sit
The colours might all come away on your lip
So I'll wait for the wild rose that's waitin' for me
In the place where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea

You remember young Diddy McClaren, of course
But he's over here with the rest of the force
I saw him one day as he stood on the strand
Stopped all the traffic with a wave of his hand
As we were talking of days that are gone
The whole town of London stood there to look on
But for all his great powers, he's wishful like me
To be back where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea.

Klaar om je Ierse wandelavontuur te beginnen?

Laat ons de details verzorgen — accommodatie, bagagetransfers en routebeschrijvingen — zodat jij kunt genieten van elke stap van je reis door de adembenemende landschappen van Ierland.

Wandel door de Cooley & Mournes

title: Leave No Trace
content:
Leave No Trace is an Outdoor Ethics Programme designed to promote and inspire responsible outdoor recreation through education, research, and partnerships. An ethic can be defined as knowing what the right thing to do is, and doing it, even if there is no one about to see you do it.

  • Plan and Prepare!
  • Respect Farm Animals and Wildlife
  • Dispose of Waste Properly
  • Minimise the Effects of Fire
  • Be Considerate of Others
  • Leave What You Find
  • Travel and Camp on Durable Ground

Leave No Trace depends more on attitude and awareness than on rules and regulations. Techniques designed to minimise the social and environmental impacts to the outdoors are incorporated into the Leave No Trace Outdoor Ethics Education Programme as seven principles.

The Leave No Trace programme is designed to help outdoor enthusiasts value the natural environment, to understand the impact of their activities, and to enable them to make decisions to minimise that impact while still enjoying their activities with freedom.

  1. Plan and Prepare!
  2. Respect Farm Animals and Wildlife
  3. Dispose of Waste Properly
  4. Minimise the Effects of Fire
  5. Be Considerate of Others
  6. Leave What You Find
  7. Travel and Camp on Durable Ground

Plan and Prepare!

  1. Before you go check, where possible, if access is allowed and your activity is permitted in the area you wish to visit.
  2. Respect any signs, regulations, policies and special concerns for the area that you wish to visit. Permits may sometimes be needed for activities on public lands.
  3. Where possible travel by public transport or share cars; consider the availability of parking.
  4. Ensure you have the skills and equipment needed for your activity and to cope with emergencies that could arise.
  5. Check the weather forecast and always be prepared for changing weather conditions.
  6. For environmental and safety reasons, and to minimise your impact on other users, keep group numbers small; split larger parties into smaller groups.

Respect Farm Animals and Wildlife

  1. Dogs should be kept under close control and should only be brought onto hills or farmland with the landowner’s permission. Some public areas stipulate that dogs must be kept on a lead at all times, please adhere to local guidelines.
  2. Observe wild animals and birds from a distance. Avoid disturbing them, particularly at sensitive times: mating, nesting and raising young (mostly between spring and early summer).
  3. Keep wildlife wild, don’t feed wild animals or birds – our foods damage their health and leave them vulnerable to predators.
  4. Farm animals are not pets; remain at a safe distance.

Dispose of Waste Properly

  1. “If You Bring It In, Take It Out” – take home all litter and leftover food (including tea bags, fruit peels and other biodegradable foods).
  2. To dispose of solid human waste, dig a hole 15-20cms deep and at least 30m from water, campsites and tracks. Cover and disguise the hole when finished.
  3. Bring home toilet paper and hygiene products.
  4. Wash yourself or your dishes 30m away from streams or lakes and if necessary use small amounts of biodegradable soap. Bring home any solids and scatter strained dishwater.

For more information on sanitation in the outdoors read the “Where to go in the outdoors” leaflet

Minimise the Effects of Fire

  1. Fires can cause lasting impacts and be devastating to forests, natural habitats and farmland. Therefore when camping use a lightweight stove for cooking.
  2. Where fires are permitted: Use established fire rings, barbecues or create a mound fire.
  3. Keep fires small. Only use sticks from the ground that can be broken by hand. Do not use growing vegetation for use as firewood.
  4. Avoid burning plastics or other substances: which emit toxic fumes.
  5. Burn all fires to ash, put out fires completely, and then scatter cool ashes. (There are no fires allowed in the National Parks of Ireland)

Practice a Leave No Trace ethic: Make it hard for others to see or hear you and  LEAVE NO TRACE of your visit.

Be Considerate of Others

  1. Respect the people who live and work in the countryside. Park appropriately – avoid blocking gateways, forest entrances or narrow roads. Remember that farm machinery, local residents and the emergency services may need access at all times.
  2. Take care not to damage property, especially walls, fences and crops.
  3. Respect other visitors and protect the quality of their experience.
  4. Let nature’s sounds prevail. Keep noise to a minimum.

Leave What You Find

  1. Respect property. For example, farming or forestry machinery, fences, stone walls etc. Leave gates as you find them (open or closed).
  2. Preserve the past: examine – without damaging – archaeological structures, old walls and heritage artefacts, e.g. holy wells, mine workings, monuments.
  3. Conserve the present: leave rocks, flowers, plants, animals and all natural habitats as you find them. Fallen trees are a valuable wildlife habitat; do not remove or use for firewood.
  4. Avoid introducing non-native plants and animals, e.g. zebra mussels in rivers and lakes.
  5. Do not build rock cairns, structures or shelters

Travel and Camp on Durable Ground

Durable ground includes established tracks and campsites, rock, gravel, dry grasses or snow.

In popular areas:

  • Concentrate use on existing tracks and campsites.
  • To avoid further erosion, travel in single file in the middle of the track even when wet or muddy.

In more remote areas:

  • Disperse use to prevent the creation of new tracks and campsites.
  • Avoid places where impacts are just beginning to show.

If camping:

  • Protect water quality by camping at least 30m from lakes and streams.
  • Keep campsites small and discreet.

Aim to leave your campsite as you found it, or better.

Ready to Start Your Irish Walking Adventure?

Let us take care of the details — accommodation, luggage transfers, and route notes — so you can focus on enjoying every step of your journey through Ireland's stunning landscapes.

title: How to Travel to Ireland for a Walking Holiday: Complete Guide 2026
content:
✈ Complete Travel Guide

How to Travel to Ireland for a Walking Holiday

Flights, ferries, visas, airport transfers and everything you need to arrive rested and trail-ready — all in one place.

Browse Our Walking Tours →

When you choose to travel to Ireland, you'll discover its breathtaking landscapes and vibrant culture. This guide will help you with all the essential details you need for your journey to travel to Ireland.

  1. Visas & Immigration
  2. Flight Options to Ireland
  3. Ferry Options
  4. Airport to Trailhead Transfers
  5. Driving in Ireland
  6. Best Time to Visit Ireland for Walking
  7. Packing & Preparation
  8. Physical Training Timeline
  9. Jet Lag Strategies
  10. Documents & Travel Insurance
  11. Arrival Day Logistics
  12. Planning Steps: Start to Trail
  13. Final Considerations When You Travel to Ireland

On This Page

Visas & Entry  ·  Flights  ·  Ferries  ·  Airport Transfers  ·  Driving  ·  Best Season  ·  Packing  ·  Training  ·  Jet Lag  ·  Documents  ·  Arrival Day  ·  Planning Steps

To travel to Ireland, we recommend checking flight options that best suit your itinerary. There are many international flights available that make it easy to travel to Ireland.

In this comprehensive guide, we will explore how to travel to Ireland seamlessly, covering everything you need for a successful trip.

Ireland welcomes walkers from around the world. The island has excellent flight connections from major cities globally, efficient transfer options from airports to trailheads, and accommodation built specifically for walkers. The logistics of travelling to Ireland for a walking holiday are genuinely straightforward — once you know what to expect.

This guide covers everything from visas and flights to packing and jet lag, whether you are travelling from North America, Europe, Australia, or anywhere else globally.

Before you travel to Ireland, it's beneficial to research various trails and activities available during your visit.

Entry Requirements

Visas & Immigration

For those looking to travel to Ireland, remember that each region offers unique experiences, so plan your itinerary accordingly.

🇺🇸

USA, Canada & Australia

Visa-free entry for tourism up to 90 days. Your passport must be valid for the full duration of your stay.

🇪🇺

EU & UK Citizens

Simple entry with a valid full passport. UK visitors should note that a passport card is no longer sufficient.

📱

Ireland ETA (Coming Soon)

An Electronic Travel Authorisation (similar to ESTA) is being introduced. Likely a simple online process costing €7–15. Check dfa.ie before booking.

⚠ Check Before You Book

ETA requirements are still evolving. Always verify current entry rules at dfa.ie for your specific nationality before finalising flights.

As you travel to Ireland, ensure you have the necessary documents and travel insurance for a smooth experience.

Many travellers opt to travel to Ireland via air, as it offers the quickest route to explore the beautiful landscapes.

Getting Here by Air

Flight Options to Ireland

Ireland's three main international airports serve different regions. Choose the one closest to your trailhead to minimise ground travel time.

🛬

Dublin Airport

Ireland's largest hub. Most transatlantic and European routes land here. Perfect for the Wicklow Way.

🛬

Cork Airport

Ideal for the Kerry Way and Dingle Way. Fewer connections but no onward transfer needed.

🛬

Shannon Airport

Gateway to the west — good for the Burren and Wild Atlantic Way routes. Less crowded than Dublin.

Flight Booking Tips

When planning your trip, make sure to book early to secure the best deals for flights and accommodations to travel to Ireland.

  • Book 6–8 weeks ahead for the best balance of price and seat availability
  • Fly Tuesday or Wednesday — typically cheaper than weekend departures
  • Morning or afternoon arrivals give you time to settle before an early night
  • Late afternoon departures on your last day allow a final morning walk
  • Consider flight insurance — if injured on the trail, coverage protects your return flight

Typical Return Prices

Tips for planning your trip to travel to Ireland will help you adjust to local customs and make the most of your adventure.

Alternative Routes

Ferry Options

If travelling from the UK or mainland Europe, ferries offer a relaxed alternative to flying — and many walkers enjoy the crossing as part of the adventure.

Whether embarking on a solo journey or with friends, knowing how to travel to Ireland opens the door to unforgettable experiences.

Getting to the Trail

Airport to Trailhead Transfers

Dublin Airport → Wicklow Way

Cork / Shannon → Kerry Way & Dingle Way

💡 Pro Tip

Most walkers spend one night in Dublin before heading to the trailhead. It eases jet lag, lets you pick up any forgotten gear, and means you start your walk refreshed rather than rushed.

Finally, remember that every step you take while you travel to Ireland will lead you to unforgettable memories.

On the Road

Driving in Ireland

  • Left-side driving: Ireland drives on the left like the UK and Australia. Allow significant adjustment time if you are unfamiliar.
  • Narrow country roads: Rural roads are considerably narrower than North American highways — reduce your speed accordingly.
  • International Driving Permit: US travellers can drive with a US licence, but an IDP (from AAA) provides useful backup.
  • Car insurance: Rental cars include basic cover; an excess waiver (~€10–20/day) is advisable.
  • Parking: Free at trailheads and in small villages. Can be expensive in Dublin and Cork city centres.

When to Go

Best Time to Visit Ireland for Walking

Most Popular

Peak Season

June – August

Most reliable weather and longest daylight, but highest prices and busiest trails. Book 2–3 months ahead.

Sweet Spot

Shoulder Seasons

May & September–October

Excellent weather, fewer crowds, better pricing. Mid-May to June 1 and all of September are particularly ideal. Book 6–8 weeks ahead.

Experienced Only

Autumn & Winter

October – February

Dramatic skies and autumn colour, very few other walkers. Weather less predictable. For experienced walkers only.

If you're unsure about the best routes to take, our guide on how to travel to Ireland will assist you in planning your journey.

What to Bring

Packing & Preparation

Essential Walking Gear

One essential part of your adventure is to make sure you are prepared to travel to Ireland, ensuring you have everything you need.

  • 🧥 Waterproof jacket (non-negotiable)
  • 👢 Broken-in hiking boots
  • 🧦 Two pairs of walking socks
  • 🧣 Thermal layers
  • 🧢 Hat and gloves (even in summer)
  • 🌞 Sunscreen
  • 🩹 First aid basics
  • 🔌 Type G power adapter (UK plug)

The Two-Bag Approach

🎒

Day Backpack (15–20L)

Water, snacks, a waterproof layer, your camera. This is what you carry on the trail each day.

🧳

Main Luggage

Clothes and overnight kit. On a Walking Holiday Ireland tour, this is transferred between accommodations every day — you never carry it on the trail.

Get Trail-Ready

Physical Training Timeline

Ireland's long-distance trails involve 15–20 km per day with elevation gain. Moderate fitness is sufficient, but preparation makes all the difference — especially on day 3 and beyond.

Your 3-Month Training Plan

When you travel to Ireland, consider the best times to visit for optimal weather and fewer crowds.

Managing the Journey

Jet Lag Strategies

  • Arrive morning or afternoon if possible — gives you time to settle before Irish bedtime
  • Stay awake your first day — light activity and eating at local times resets your rhythm fastest
  • Walk on your first full day — physical activity is one of the most effective jet lag remedies
  • Get morning sunshine — daylight is the most powerful circadian reset available
  • Start walking after 1–2 rest days — arriving early means you begin the trail refreshed, not exhausted

Admin & Insurance

Documents & Travel Insurance

📄

Essential Documents

Valid passport · Travel insurance · Flight & accommodation confirmations · Walking tour booking · Credit cards & euros · Driver's licence if hiring a car

☁️

Digital Backups

Store copies of your passport, insurance, and all confirmations in a cloud service accessible offline in an emergency.

🛡️

Travel Insurance

Strongly recommended. Covers trip cancellation, emergency evacuation, lost luggage, and medical expenses. Typically €30–80 for a 1–2 week trip.

When You Land

Arrival Day Logistics

Resist the urge to start walking immediately. Two easy days before your first trail day transforms the entire experience.

Day 1

Arrival Day

  • Clear immigration & collect bags
  • Transfer to accommodation
  • Early dinner and rest
  • Early night — resist jet lag temptation

Day 2

Orientation Day

  • Light walk or local exploration
  • Finalise gear and layers
  • Good meal and early night
  • Review route maps and notes

Day 3

Trail Start 🥾

  • Begin your walking holiday
  • Full energy, adjusted rhythm
  • Enjoy every single step

Your Roadmap

Planning Steps: Start to Trail

  1. Choose your trail — Kerry Way, Dingle Way, Wicklow Way, Barrow Way, Causeway Coast, or a combination.
  2. Decide your dates — May–September is optimal. Book 6–8 weeks ahead at minimum.
  3. Book your flights — Secure airfares early; prices rise significantly within 4 weeks of departure.
  4. Book your walking tour — This locks in accommodation, luggage transfers, and route notes in one step.
  5. Arrange airport transfers — Co-ordinate with your tour operator or book independently.
  6. Sort your documentation — ETA if required, travel insurance, and digital backups of all confirmations.
  7. Begin your training — Follow the 3-month plan above. Your legs will thank you on day 3 of the Kerry Way.
  8. Pack strategically — Day pack for the trail, main luggage for transfers. Lighter is always better.
  9. Arrive, rest, and walk — Two easy days before the trail. Then enjoy every step of Ireland's finest landscapes.

Self-Guided Walking Holidays

Ready to Start Your Irish Walking Holiday?

We take care of accommodation, luggage transfers, and route notes — so you can focus entirely on the walking.

View All Walking Tours →

Popular Routes

  • Kerry Way — 8 Days from €1,025
  • Dingle Way — 8 Days from €985
  • Wicklow Way — 8 Days from €1,029
  • Causeway Coast — 8 Days from €959
  • Barrow Way — 8 Days from €859

About Walking Holiday Ireland

We specialise in fully supported self-guided walking holidays on Ireland's finest long-distance trails. All accommodation is pre-booked, your luggage is transferred between guesthouses every morning, and detailed route notes are included — so you can focus entirely on the scenery, the villages, and the trail.

Get in Touch

📞 +353 42 932 3396

📧 info@walkingholidayireland.com

💬 WhatsApp us

Final Considerations When You Travel to Ireland

  1. To make the most of your walking holiday, learn how to travel to Ireland efficiently by following our tips.
  2. Don't forget to check local transport options available once you travel to Ireland to enhance your experience.
  3. With careful planning, your decision to travel to Ireland will lead you to breathtaking scenery and rich culture.
  4. Once you arrive, the excitement of your journey will begin as you start to travel to Ireland's stunning landscapes.
  5. As you settle in, the joy of travel to Ireland will become apparent through its warm hospitality and vibrant atmosphere.
  6. In the following sections, we will delve deeper into how to efficiently travel to Ireland and explore its hidden gems.

title: Want to get your family hiking?
content:
Here are our tips for a great day out on the trails with the family on family hiking holidays in Ireland.

  • Family Hiking Holidays
  • The value of family hiking.
  • Family Hiking is quality time together.
  • Nine great tips on getting hiking & outdoors holidays to suit all age groups.
  1. Family Hiking Holidays
  2. The value of family hiking.
  3. Family Hiking is quality time together.
  4. Nine great tips on getting hiking & outdoors holidays to suit all age groups.

Family Hiking Holidays

Where to begin?

We all know exercise has tremendous value to both our physical and mental health. Getting outdoors, whatever the weather, not only revitalises us but has the natural benefit of releasing the feel-good hormones, endorphin, serotonin and dopamine. These are all good reasons to get your family hiking. And if you’re lucky enough to have beautiful scenery on your doorstep, that’s a definite plus!

Thankfully I am lucky to have the beautiful Cooley mountains, not entirely on my doorstep but only a short drive away. Therefore the beauty of living in Ireland means a short drive in any direction will give you access to nature, whether it's mountains, hills, rivers or sea.

The value of family hiking.

For my family hiking has been of tremendous value to us. We have made beautiful memories over the years. At times yes, there have been challenges, especially when the children were younger and the track may have been testing and difficult. One thing is for sure, at the end of these hikes, we all feel a sense of accomplishment, a satisfaction that few other activities would equal, that sense of being together, of encouraging each other to achieve our shared goal of getting to the top of that mountain!.

Family Hiking is quality time together.

For us, spending time together without phones, iPad’s and the internet, has given us the gift of not only being in each other’s presence but of quiet moments of conversation that we would never have had if we hadn’t pushed ourselves up the hills to try something different.

My advice to those who look past the mountains and hills close to where they live, try them out, go for it! With a little bit of research, you could be at the top of that mountain that you have been looking at for years. You could be there with your family, enjoying the scenery and the challenge, doing something together, making your memories!. I have a few tips for the novice hiker who wants to get up into those hills!

Nine great tips on getting hiking & outdoors holidays to suit all age groups.

  1. Start with an easy looped trail – people are often put off by mountain routes, but interestingly many mountain routes start in forests. My advice is to start short and slow and gradually build on this. There are six national parklands in Ireland, and these are a wonderful resource with many short looped walks, perfect for the family who want to get out and about in nature. See  www.npws.ie for further information.
  2. Research local walking trails and forest walks. Coillte – the Irish forestry organisation provides information on local walks with free trail maps to download.  For more information see www.coillte.ie
  3. Make it fun. Walking with other family members or friends is a great way to make the walk interesting for children. Playing games to keep them occupied on the route such as ‘I spy with my little eye’ and ‘20 questions’ are great ways to distract and amuse children, and can be played by all the family as you walk. Hiking with friends who have children the same age as yours can work out well too.
  4. Geocaching- fun for the whole family!  Treasure hunting with a difference. To play, all you need to do is download the geocaching app and a GPS device. This will allow you to locate carefully hidden containers with a small treasure called a geocache. The beauty of this game is that once you find the geocache, you return it for the next person to find. Anyone can play, you can even hide your own treasure for other people to locate. There are 808 geocaches around Louth.  For more information, have a look at www.geocaching.com
  5. Bring a picnic- plenty of snacks and fluids to keep the children motivated and happy! Nothing like a picnic when you reach the summit or are halfway through the walk.
  6. Bring essential equipment such as a first aid box containing plasters, antiseptic cream, sterile water / wet wipes and tissues. A space blanket is handy to have and weighs nothing at all. Always prepare for the unexpected.
  7. Haversacks- encourage all members of the family to carry their water and snacks. As a result, this gives ownership and helps build confidence and responsibility in your child.
  8. Dress for the weather- Ensure that the children wear high vis. safety vests so that they be safe and be seen. Walking boots are a good investment and a waterproof/ showerproof jacket for any unexpected rain showers. I would also recommend always hiking with full arm and leg cover whatever the weather, but especially warm humid weather. This will cut down on insect bites/ midges and if walking off track, protect you from ticks. https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/government/uploads/system/uploads/attachment_data/file/694158/PHE_Tick_Leaflet.pdf
  9. Leave no trace. Always be mindful that whatever you take with you on the trail, that you also remove, even when it comes to banana skins. This will ensure that you leave nothing behind. http://www.leavenotraceireland.org/

As well as the vast personal benefits of hiking with your family, there are enormous benefits to your child's development. It increases physical activity, supports creativity and problem solving, teaches a lifelong skill, improves social relations, and enhances cognitive abilities. What is there not to like about hiking? The benefits are enormous, give it a go! This is the perfect time of the year to plan your walks as a family. So, go on, go for it and create your wonderful memories!

Ready to Start Your Irish Walking Adventure?

Let us take care of the details — accommodation, luggage transfers, and route notes — so you can focus on enjoying every step of your journey through Ireland's stunning landscapes.

View All Walking Tours
excerpt: We all know exercise has tremendous value to both our physical and mental health. Getting outdoors, whatever the weather, not only revitalises us but has the natural benefit of releasing the feel-good hormones, endorphin, serotonin and dopamine. These are all good reasons to get your family hiking. And if you’re lucky enough to have beautiful scenery on your doorstep, that’s a definite plus!
author: Cliff

title: Discover Blackrock: County Louth's Seaside Treasure
content:
Discover Blackrock: County Louth's Seaside Treasure

Have you been searching for that perfect Irish coastal getaway that combines stunning scenery with authentic charm? Look no further than Blackrock village in County Louth, one of Ireland's most delightful hidden gems along the Ancient East coastline.

  • Discover Blackrock: County Louth's Seaside Treasure
  • A Seaside Village with Character
  • Beach Life In County Louth
  • The Heart of Village Life: The Promenade
  • From Fishing Village to Coastal Destination
  • A Haven for Nature Lovers
  • Seasonal Celebrations
  • Walking Routes and Explorations
  • Plan Your Visit to Blackrock
  • Discover the Magic of Blackrock

A Seaside Village with Character

Blackrock sits beautifully on the shores of Dundalk Bay, with the impressive Cooley Mountains creating a dramatic backdrop. This seaside village gets its name from the distinctive dark rocks that dot its shoreline, creating peaceful coves and fascinating rock pools that captivate visitors of all ages.

Just south of Dundalk and conveniently located near the M1 motorway, Blackrock offers the perfect midpoint between Dublin and Belfast. This strategic location makes it an ideal base for exploring Ireland's Ancient East while enjoying the laid-back atmosphere of coastal living.

Beach Life In County Louth

The beaches of the village offer something truly special among Ireland's coastal destinations. The main beach stretches along the village centre's promenade, providing excellent public access and amenities for day visitors.

For those seeking more secluded spots, Priest's Beach, just below the church and the quieter Ladies Beach to the north, offer perfect alternatives.

What makes Blackrock Beaches particularly fascinating is its remarkable tidal range. The shoreline features an unusually gentle gradient, with the tide retreating about 5 kilometres during low tide. This natural phenomenon reveals extensive sand and mud flats, creating an ever-changing landscape that adds to the village's unique appeal.

"The beaches here change completely from morning to evening," explains local resident Mary Kearney. "You can walk out for what seems like miles at low tide and then watch as the water transforms the landscape completely just hours later."

The Heart of Village Life: The Promenade

The promenade serves as the social hub of the village, offering panoramic views across Dundalk Bay. Here you'll find charming seafront cafés, traditional Irish pubs, and quality restaurants serving everything from freshly caught seafood to international cuisine.

The vibrant promenade comes alive during summer evenings when locals and visitors gather to enjoy the sea air and spectacular sunsets. Vintage shops and specialty stores line the street, perfect for picking up unique souvenirs or locally crafted items.

The vibrant promenade comes alive during summer evenings when locals and visitors gather to enjoy the sea air and spectacular sunsets. Vintage shops and specialty stores line the street, perfect for picking up unique souvenirs or locally crafted items.

At the centre of the promenade stands Blackrock's defining symbol—the Millennium Sundial. This impressive bronze sculpture, "Aisling" (meaning "dream" or "vision" in Irish), is the work of local artist Tanya Elliot Nyegaard. It holds the distinction of being Ireland's largest sundial in a public space.

From Fishing Village to Coastal Destination

Blackrock has transformed dramatically over the centuries. What began as a tiny fishing village in the 18th century evolved into a popular seaside resort following World War II. Local landlord Thomas Fortescue established the settlement around 1752, but it was during the Victorian times that Blackrock began developing as a recreational destination.

Thomas Fortescue - Lord Clermont was lord of Clermont Park. He was very severe on the men who worked for him. It is said that they had to be in before the lark rose in the morning. One morning, a man was running to work, and he met a priest who asked him why he was running. The man told him that he had to be in before the lark rose, and the priest said, "May a lark never be seen in Clermont again." From that day, it is said no lark was seen during Lord Clermont's time. - Mr. Loughran

The 1950s and 1960s saw Blackrock emerge as a popular holiday resort for families from Dundalk and beyond. In 1962, the village opened an outdoor seawater swimming pool that became the community's pride, though it has since been replaced by residential developments overlooking the sea.

Today, Blackrock maintains its seaside charm while offering modern amenities that make it perfect for contemporary travellers seeking authentic Irish experiences without overwhelming crowds.

A Haven for Nature Lovers

Dundalk Bay holds special environmental status as both a Special Area of Conservation and a Special Protection Area. These designations highlight the bay's importance to wildlife, particularly birds.

The extensive salt marshes and mud flats create perfect feeding grounds for diverse bird species, making Blackrock a paradise for birdwatchers. Visitors can spot oystercatchers, Brent geese, dunlins, and redshank in their natural habitat throughout the year, with seasonal migrations bringing different species to observe.

For those interested in water sports, Blackrock has developed a reputation as an excellent sailboarding venue when conditions are right. Windsurfers dot the bay on breezy days, adding color and energy to the coastal views.

Seasonal Celebrations

Blackrock hosts vibrant community events throughout the year. The annual Pumpkin Festival brings creative displays and family activities to the village, while the traditional Stephen's Day swim sees brave locals plunge into the Irish Sea for charity during the winter holidays.

Blackrock hosts vibrant community events throughout the year. The annual Pumpkin Festival brings creative displays and family activities to the village, while the traditional Stephen's Day swim sees brave locals plunge into the Irish Sea for charity during the winter holidays.

The Blackrock Tidy Towns committee organizes regular community initiatives that have earned the village recognition for its cleanliness and community spirit. These events reflect the warm, welcoming nature of Blackrock and provide entertainment for visitors across different seasons.

Walking Routes and Explorations

With its position at the northern end of Ireland's Ancient East, Blackrock serves as an excellent starting point for walking enthusiasts. Numerous circular routes are available both within the village and along the scenic coastline.

More adventurous hikers can explore routes into the Cooley Mountains for rewarding panoramic vistas. Local hiking groups regularly organise group walks suitable for various fitness levels.

Plan Your Visit to Blackrock

The best time to visit Blackrock depends on your preferences. Summer brings warmer temperatures and bustling village life, while shoulder seasons offer quieter experiences with equally beautiful coastal views. Even winter has its charm, with dramatic seascapes and cosy pubs providing warm refuge from the elements.

Blackrock is easily accessible by car from both Dublin (about 1 hour) and Belfast (about 1 hour 15 minutes) via the M1 motorway. Public transportation options are also available from Dundalk, which has good rail and bus connections to major cities.

Discover the Magic of Blackrock

With its stunning location, rich history, and warm community spirit, Blackrock village offers the perfect introduction to County Louth and Ireland's Ancient East. Whether you're watching rare birds along the shoreline, enjoying fresh seafood with ocean views, or simply breathing in the rejuvenating sea air, this charming seaside village delivers authentic travel experiences that create lasting memories.

Pack your walking shoes, bring your camera, and prepare to discover one of Ireland's most delightful coastal treasures – Blackrock awaits!

Planning Your Trip:

  • Best time to visit: May through September for milder weather
  • Don't miss: Sunset views from the promenade
  • Local tip: Check tide times to experience the full beauty of Blackrock beach
  • Nearby attractions: Cooley Peninsula, Carlingford, and historic Dundalk

Welcome to Blackrock – where the beauty of Ireland's east coast comes alive!
excerpt: The picturesque seaside village of Blackrock with its tranquil coves and rock pools lies on the shores of Dundalk Bay and the foot of the Cooley Mountains in County Louth.  Blackrock is conveniently placed just south of Dundalk close to the M1 motorway halfway between Dublin and Belfast
author: Cliff

title: The Best Hillwalking Locations in Ireland
content:
Index

Our Top 5 Best Hillwalking Locations in Ireland
The Dingle Way: Hiking in the West of Ireland
The Kerry Way on the Wild Atlantic Way
Hiking the Wicklow Way
Hiking the Glens of Antrim and Causeway Coast  
Follow the Beara Way hiking trail.
Best Hillwalking Locations in Ireland we also have to mention

The Best hillwalking locations in Ireland offer something unique that other destinations cannot match. Sure, Switzerland offers higher peaks and jodeling maidens. Italy offers excellent coffee before you start your walk and possibly the best pasta in the evening to revitalise yourself after a hard day on the trails.

Ireland, oh Ireland, it is truly exceptional. It not only presents diverse and untamed terrains, ranging from rough coastlines to winding emerald valleys adorned with azure lakes that mirror the untamed heavens and violet heathers. Upon straying from the best multi day hikes in Ireland, one is greeted by warm and hospitable family-operated bed & breakfasts, where captivating tales of local traditions and myths are shared. Additionally, some pubs serve delectable meals and the finest pint of Guinness or local whiskey one could desire.

On top of all that, Hiking in Ireland is a deep and spiritual experience that cannot be matched and has to be experienced at least once in a lifetime. Check out our top hiking tips here.. I am willing to bet that one walking holiday won't be enough, and you will be back for more!

There are many ways you can experience hillwalking in Ireland. By yourself, with family and friends, on hillwalk tours or with a walking club. So, there is no reason not to explore Ireland's tracks and trails and enjoy all that is on offer!

Our Top 5 Best Hillwalking Locations in Ireland

Below we have selected some of our Best Hillwalking Locations in Ireland. What is your favourite, and which ones have you done or would you like to do?

1) The Dingle Way: Hiking in the West of Ireland

Situated in the southwest of Ireland, the tour starts in the capital of Kerry, Tralee town. It is one of the 30 long-distance walking trails and one of the best hiking trails in Ireland. On average, it would take an adult to walk the trail 8-9 days as the trail is 179km.

The Dingle Way is famous for its many different landscapes. It doesn’t take long before you see a massive change in scenery. The walking tour follows mountain trails, green roads, old tracks, and often the foothills of the mountains. The Dingle Way is the gateway to the Dingle Peninsula. 

2) The Kerry Way on the Wild Atlantic Way

The Kerry Way is a fantastic scenic long-distance walking trail in County Kerry. The walk is approximately 215km long and takes, on average, between 6 to 12 days to complete.

The walk starts in Killarney, with stunning lakes, hidden valleys, panoramic ocean views, mountain backdrops, and so much more.

Many towns pass through on the Kerry Way route. Some include Kenmare, Glenbeigh, Waterville, and so many more. This is an excellent walking tour as it caters for all interests.  

3) Hiking the Wicklow Way

Wicklow lies south of Dublin and is often nicknamed the Garden of Ireland. The Wicklow Way is one of Ireland's oldest way-marked long-distance hill walk tours, and most of it runs through the Wicklow Mountains National Park. At 127km, it ascends some 3,200 meters along its entire journey.

Walking the entire Wicklow Way can take 5 to 8 days, depending on your fitness and speed. Walking Holiday Ireland offers self-guided tours from a 5-day tour, walking part of the Wicklow way, or we can provide a custom (tailor-made) tour that suits your personal requirements.

The Wicklow National Park offers beautiful scenery and some truly remote upland areas. These include fast-flowing mountain streams, farmland, forests, mountains, and steep-sided glacial valleys. Many wild animals call Wicklow home, including the fox, rabbit, deer, goat, hare, badger, and red squirrel. Many different tree types, such as chestnut, beech, oak, larch, and many more, can be seen throughout the uplands. 

4) Hiking the Glens of Antrim and Causeway Coast  

Discover this beautiful part of Northern Ireland, which is home to the famous Giant’s Causeway. It is full of friendly people, stunning landscapes, and inspirational locations. 3 areas of designation have incredible natural beauty, nine glens which include Glengariff, the queen of the glens, secluded coastal tracks, wonderful forest parks, and quaint fishing villages. Along with the world-renowned giant’s causeway, other amazing spots exist, including Dunluce and Dunseverick Castle.  

5) Follow the Beara Way hiking trail.

The Beara Way begins and ends in Glengariff in Cork. It is a long circular route with a distance of 152km around the Beara peninsula. It is considered one of the best multi-day hikes in Ireland. The route passes through seacoast scenery and magnificent rugged mountains in Cork and Kerry. Beara is a world apart and offers peacefulness and tranquillity that has long since vanished in other parts. The Beara Way follows country roads, old green roads, and boggy mountain trails. It is well signposted and marked and is one of Ireland’s best-kept secrets.

Best Hillwalking Locations in Ireland we also have to mention

If you are looking for some of the best hillwalking locations in Ireland, you have plenty of options to choose from. Ireland has stunning landscapes, rugged mountains, scenic coastlines, diverse flora and fauna. Here are some of the other locations you should consider for your next hillwalking adventure:

  • The Burren Way: This trail takes you through the unique limestone landscape of the Burren region in County Clare, where you can admire the rich biodiversity, ancient monuments and spectacular views of the Atlantic Ocean. You will also pass by the famous Cliffs of Moher, one of the most iconic sights in Ireland.
  • The Sheep's Head Way: This trail follows the coastline of the Sheep's Head Peninsula in West Cork, a remote and unspoilt area with stunning scenery and wildlife. You will walk along cliffs, bays, headlands and valleys and discover the lighthouses, stone circles and castles that dot the landscape.

Why not visit one of Ireland's beautiful National Parks?

What are some of the highest peaks in Ireland?

The highest peak in Ireland is Carrauntoohil (1,039m) in County Kerry, which is part of the macgillycuddy’s Reeks range.
Other high peaks in the same range include Beenkeragh (1,010m), Caher (1,001m), Cnoc na Péiste (988m), and Maolán Buí (973m). The highest peak in Leinster is Lugnaquillia Mountain (925m) in County Wicklow, while the highest peak in Munster is Galtymore Mountain (918m) on the border of Tipperary and Limerick.
The highest peak in Ulster is Slieve Donard (850m) in County Down, and the highest peak in Connacht is Mweelrea (814m) in County Mayo.

What are some of the best coastal walks in Ireland?

Ireland has a long and varied coastline, offering many opportunities for scenic walks along cliffs, beaches and islands. Some of the best coastal walks include the Bray to Greystones cliff walk in County Wicklow, which follows a well-marked path along the Irish Sea and offers views of marine life.
Another popular coastal walk is the Faha Ridge on Brandon Mountain in County Kerry. This challenging but rewarding route takes stunning views of the Atlantic Ocean and the Dingle Peninsula. For a more island experience, you can walk around Masatiompan on the Blasket Islands, which are rich in wildlife and cultural heritage.

What are some of the best long-distance hiking trails in Ireland?

If you are looking for a more adventurous and immersive experience, you can try one of the many long-distance hiking trails that crisscross Ireland. These trails are usually waymarked and can be done in sections or as a whole. One of the most famous and popular trails is the Wicklow Way, which stretches for 127km from Dublin to Carlow and passes through some of the most beautiful landscapes in Ireland, including mountains, lakes, valleys and forests.
excerpt: Ireland offers the best hillwalking locations you can imagine. From coastal walks on rugged cliffs or white sandy beaches to national parks and deep green valleys.
author: Cliff

title: How to choose the right pair of hiking boots
content:
Icy-cold and wet or hot and dry?

Hot, dry climates – you will need some boots that will keep your feet fresh and sweat-free. Now the focus is on airflow, not on waterproofing.

Icy-cold and wet climates – you will need waterproof, well-insulated boots. Crampons that are flexible are a must.

Types of cuts and hiking boot styles

Because hiking boots come in different cuts, you should pick a pair that is ideal for your kind of adventure:

  • Low cut – for lightweight, casual hiking. These boots are best worn on well-maintained trails. In this case, the best is light hiking shoes, which are perfect for day hiking.
  • Mid-cut – more ankle support and balance for the ones who are going to deviate on more difficult terrains (backpacking boots – built for the multi-day trek for a variety of terrain)
  • High-cut – the best for adventurous hikers, who are thinking about exploring potentially tenuous ground. (mountaineering boots – designed for rocky territory, glacial trails, snowy paths).

Hope this little guide will help you find the hiking boots that are going to be your best companion on any hiking tour. If you choose smartly, they will take you for sure on many adventures!

Stay safe and see you on the trail!

Ready to Start Your Irish Walking Adventure?

Let us take care of the details — accommodation, luggage transfers, and route notes — so you can focus on enjoying every step of your journey through Ireland's stunning landscapes.

The terrain importance

To get the ideal pair of boots for hiking, you will need to understand better the terrain you are going to explore. If you are hiking in rough terrain, you will need a pair of hiking boots with gaiters – these will cover the vulnerable tops of your footwear to protect your feet from the elements entirely. Gaiters for hiking are usually lightweight and breathable and will offer the necessary protection against rocks, grit and light rain.

If you are a light hiker, you should seek out a boot that feels light on the feet. These types of boots are comparable to hiking shoes with a higher ankle shaft height.

Icy-cold and wet or hot and dry?

Hot, dry climates – you will need some boots that will keep your feet fresh and sweat-free. Now the focus is on airflow, not on waterproofing.

Icy-cold and wet climates – you will need waterproof, well-insulated boots. Crampons that are flexible are a must.

Types of cuts and hiking boot styles

Because hiking boots come in different cuts, you should pick a pair that is ideal for your kind of adventure:

  • Low cut – for lightweight, casual hiking. These boots are best worn on well-maintained trails. In this case, the best is light hiking shoes, which are perfect for day hiking.
  • Mid-cut – more ankle support and balance for the ones who are going to deviate on more difficult terrains (backpacking boots – built for the multi-day trek for a variety of terrain)
  • High-cut – the best for adventurous hikers, who are thinking about exploring potentially tenuous ground. (mountaineering boots – designed for rocky territory, glacial trails, snowy paths).

Hope this little guide will help you find the hiking boots that are going to be your best companion on any hiking tour. If you choose smartly, they will take you for sure on many adventures!

Stay safe and see you on the trail!

What’s the ideal fit for hiking shoes & boots?

If your feet don’t move around when you walk, but you can wiggle your toes without them touching the front of the shoe, you’ve found the right pair of boots for you! Wiggle your toes and start your adventure!

It is crucial to try the boots with a pair of socks similar to the ones you intend to wear on your adventure. Socks can have a significant influence on the overall fit of the boots, that’s why you should consider hiking socks because they are going to provide you with added cushioning and temperature control. A big plus: they are anti-bacterial!

  • Durability
  • Weight
  • Stability
  • Warmth
  • Water resistance

author: Cliff

title: Hiking in Donegal: Slieve League and Mount Errigal
content:
Practical Essentials: Getting Here, When to Go, Where to Stay

Getting to Donegal

By Car: The most practical option. Donegal is 3–4 hours' drive from Dublin, 2 hours from Derry. Roads are good but narrow in places; allow extra time in winter or poor visibility.

By Bus: Bus Éireann and GoBus operate routes from Dublin and Derry to Donegal town (the main hub). Journey times are long (5+ hours from Dublin) but affordable. From Donegal town, local buses serve main villages, though connections to trailheads can be limited. Combine with local taxis for mountain access.

Where to Base Yourself:

  • **Donegal Town:** The obvious hub. Good accommodation range, restaurants, pubs, and bus connections. Slightly touristy but genuinely welcoming.
  • **Carrick:** Closer to Slieve League (ideal if that's your focus). Smaller, quieter, excellent local accommodation and restaurants.
  • **Gweedore/Dunlewy:** Central for Errigal and Glenveagh routes. More Irish-language area; authentic feel; good walking community.
  • **Glencolmcille:** Perfect base for coastal routes. Remote, beautiful, simple accommodation. Genuinely feels like the edge of Ireland.

For multi-day walking holidays, we arrange luggage transfers between accommodation, meaning you walk at your own pace without carrying a heavy pack. It transforms the experience, especially over multiple days.

Best Season

  • **Summer (June–August):** Longest days, most reliable weather (though rain is still frequent), busiest trailheads. Midges can be annoying in valleys.
  • **Autumn (September–October):** Ideal season. Weather improving, fewer crowds, spectacular colours, and clearer air. September is often the driest month.
  • **Spring (April–May):** Lengthening days, wildflowers (especially bluebells and rhododendrons), greening landscape. Weather still unpredictable.
  • **Winter (November–March):** Shortest days, harshest weather, but dramatic clouds and light. Beautiful for experienced walkers; challenging for others.

What to Pack

Donegal weather is Atlantic-facing and genuinely changeable. Pack:

  • Waterproof jacket and trousers (essential, not optional)
  • Windproof layers
  • Warm mid-layer fleece or merino
  • Waterproof pack cover
  • Good hiking boots with ankle support (scree and boggy sections demand them)
  • Spare socks and hat
  • Sunscreen and sunglasses (UV is strong at altitude, even on overcast days)
  • Waterproof bag for phone and essentials
  • Snacks and water bottle

Weather can change in minutes. The principle is "prepare for the worst, hope for the best."

Getting Around While Hiking

If you're doing a multi-day walking holiday with luggage transfers, you simply walk to your next accommodation each day. We handle packing, transportation, and logistics—you carry only a small daypack (with lunch, water, and essentials).

For day hikes, arrange transport via local taxi firms or (if you're driving) leave a car at your endpoint and arrange collection. Some walks can be done as out-and-back routes if you prefer returning to the same accommodation.

Practical Tips for Hiking in Donegal

Safety Considerations

  • **Weather:** Conditions change rapidly. Always check forecasts before heading out, but expect them to be partly wrong. Visibility can drop from excellent to near-zero within minutes.
  • **Navigation:** Trails are generally waymarked, but in poor visibility, a map and compass (or GPS) are essential. Phone signal is patchy at altitude.
  • **Bog Walking:** Much of Donegal is bog. Paths can be wet and slippery. Bog is slower than grass—allow extra time. Never try to "shortcut" across bog if the path disappears; return to your last known point and navigate carefully.
  • **Mountain Streams:** After rain, streams become torrents. Crossings that are trivial in dry weather become dangerous. Don't attempt to cross fast water.

Making the Most of Your Walking Holiday

  • **Start with One Man's Pass:** If you do Slieve League, the ridge is unforgettable. It's exposed but safe; the scramble is manageable.
  • **Climb Errigal Early:** It's popular, so ascending before 10 am means summit views without crowds.
  • **Discover Glenveagh Slowly:** Don't rush through the park. Walk the easier Doon Level Trail one day, Spinetail the next. The park reveals itself gradually.
  • **Talk to Locals:** Donegal walkers are genuinely helpful. They'll give you honest advice about conditions, hidden routes, and excellent places to eat.

Ready to Explore Donegal?

Hiking in Donegal is about more than collecting peaks. It's about standing on sea cliffs watching Atlantic storms approach, climbing mountains that feel genuinely wild, and discovering valleys where sheep outnumber people. It's the kind of walking holiday that reminds you why you fell in love with mountains in the first place.

If you're planning a Donegal walking holiday and want to walk at your own pace without worrying about logistics, we offer self-guided routes across the region with full support—accommodation arranged in handpicked B&Bs, luggage transfers handled, and detailed route notes provided. You carry only essentials and focus on enjoying every step.

Whether this is your first visit to Donegal or you're returning to explore new routes, these trails are calling. The question isn't whether you can do them—it's when you'll start planning your trip. Let's get you to Donegal's peaks and cliffs.

Ready to start planning your Donegal walking holiday? Browse our Donegal walking tours and discover why these mountains matter so deeply to Irish walkers.

Ready to Start Your Irish Walking Adventure?

Let us take care of the details — accommodation, luggage transfers, and route notes — so you can focus on enjoying every step of your journey through Ireland's stunning landscapes.

Walk the Wicklow Way

The Hidden Gems: Beyond the Classics

Glenveagh National Park Routes

Glenveagh National Park deserves its own article (and we've written one if you want deep detail), but it's essential to mention here because it offers something different from Slieve League and Errigal. Instead of exposed peaks, Glenveagh delivers deep, green valleys, clear mountain rivers, and genuine solitude.

The Doon Level Trail (3 km, 1.5 hours, easy):

This is Glenveagh at its most accessible. A waymarked path follows an old estate road through native woodland, alongside the pristine Doon River. It's flat, scenic, and perfect for walkers seeking something gentler or families with children. The river views are peaceful; the woodland feels genuinely secluded. In summer, the glen is emerald green; in autumn, the colours are extraordinary.

The Spinetail Trail (6 km, 3–4 hours, moderate):

For something more substantial, this circular route climbs from glen floor to open moorland, offering panoramic views across the park's mountains and down to Lough Veagh. The upper sections are exposed; weather and visibility matter here. But on clear days, the 360-degree views make it feel like you're on top of the world. Walkers describe it as the route that best captures Glenveagh's scale and grandeur.

Why Glenveagh Matters: It's where Irish wilderness feels intimate rather than intimidating. The park has excellent infrastructure (visitor centres, facilities), but the wild landscape feels completely preserved. If you're doing a multi-day Donegal walking holiday, Glenveagh should anchor one or two days.

For full details on Glenveagh routes and logistics, check out our complete Glenveagh National Park hiking guide.

The Donegal Bay Coastal Paths

The coastline west of Slieve League offers quieter coastal walking with dramatically different character. The cliffs here are lower but equally beautiful, and the paths feel more like genuine trails than the more popular routes.

Malinbeg to Glencolmcille (10 km, 4–5 hours, moderate):

This coastal walk winds along sea cliffs, through hidden coves, and past ancient archaeological sites. The path dips and climbs repeatedly (hence the duration for distance), but the views are magical. You're walking past sea-stacks, looking down at wild-looking coves, and crossing moorland with unobstructed Atlantic views. It's less crowded than Slieve League but equally spectacular. The village of Glencolmcille at the end has excellent pubs and restaurants—perfect for refuelling after the walk.

Fintra Head Loop (7 km, 3–4 hours, moderate):

West of Glencolmcille, this loop climbs toward headland with sweeping coastal views, then descends to a wild pebble beach. It's the kind of route where you'll likely meet more sheep than walkers. The weather changes dramatically out here; get this wrong, and it's surprisingly exposed. But on clear days, it's magical.

Walk the Wicklow Way

The Classic Routes: What Makes Each Special

Slieve League: The Dramatic Coastal Climb

Slieve League (Sliabh Liag) isn't just Donegal's highest sea cliff—it's one of Ireland's most cinematogenic walking routes, and the reputation is completely justified. The cliffs rise 601 metres straight from the Atlantic, making them nearly twice the height of their famous Cliffs of Moher cousins. But it's not just about the vertical drama; it's the sense of exposure, the howling Atlantic wind, and those moments when you round a ridge and the landscape simply stops, dropping away into blue.

The Classic Slieve League Ridge Walk (8–9 km, 4–5 hours, moderate to strenuous):

This is the walker's choice. Rather than tackling it from below (a steep, boggy slog), most experienced hikers start from the Cliff Edge carpark and traverse the dramatic ridge, descending toward Carrigan Head. You'll encounter One Man's Pass—a narrow, exposed section that looks genuinely intimidating but is perfectly manageable with care and common sense. The scrambling is minimal; the exposure is maximum. Walkers with a head for heights will find it exhilarating; those prone to vertigo might prefer approaching from below.

The coastal views are astonishing: you're looking down at storm-carved caves, out toward Tory Island and the Aran Islands, and back across Donegal Bay. The ridge walk captures what makes Donegal special—genuine wildness, minimal infrastructure, and the feeling that you're walking somewhere few people venture.

Slieve League Loop (10 km, 5–6 hours, strenuous):

If you want the full experience without the scramble, the longer loop combines the ridge traverse with gentler moorland walking. You'll climb from Carrigan Head via open bog, cross high plateaus with 360-degree views, and descend back toward the coast. It's tougher underfoot (bog sections can be wet and slow) but equally rewarding.

Getting to Slieve League: Head to Carrick (a small village in southwest Donegal) and follow signs toward Teelin. The Cliff Edge carpark is the trailhead. Allow 1.5 hours' drive from Donegal town. The road narrows significantly as you approach; in peak season, carpark capacity is limited. Going early is wise.

Mount Errigal: The Signature Cone

Mount Errigal (Portuguese: An Earagail) is Donegal's most iconic mountain—that distinctive conical peak you've probably seen in photographs, sitting alone in the northeast of the county. At 752 metres, it's not the highest in Donegal, but its isolated position and perfect cone shape make it feel grander than its altitude suggests.

The Direct Ascent (5 km, 2.5–3.5 hours, moderate):

From the Dunlewy carpark, a well-trodden but steep path climbs the northeast flank, gaining 700 metres of elevation. The lower slopes are grassy, but the upper mountain is scree and loose rock—watch your footing, especially on descent. The reward is a summit with views stretching across Donegal's interior mountains, westward to the Atlantic, and on clear days across to the Scottish coast.

The mountain attracts weekend crowds (especially in summer), but it's busy rather than overcrowded. Start early if you prefer solitude. The scree descent is fun and quick; many walkers actually enjoy the descent more than the climb.

Why Errigal Matters: It's approachable enough for confident intermediate walkers, dramatic enough to feel genuinely mountainous, and iconic enough that summiting it becomes a true memory marker. If you're spending a walking holiday in Donegal, Errigal absolutely deserves a day.

Getting to Errigal: Head toward Gweedore (An Gaoth Dobhair) in central Donegal. The Dunlewy carpark is well signposted. About 45 minutes from Donegal town.

  • **Start with One Man's Pass:** If you do Slieve League, the ridge is unforgettable. It's exposed but safe; the scramble is manageable.
  • **Climb Errigal Early:** It's popular, so ascending before 10 am means summit views without crowds.
  • **Discover Glenveagh Slowly:** Don't rush through the park. Walk the easier Doon Level Trail one day, Spinetail the next. The park reveals itself gradually.
  • **Talk to Locals:** Donegal walkers are genuinely helpful. They'll give you honest advice about conditions, hidden routes, and excellent places to eat.

Ready to Explore Donegal?

Hiking in Donegal is about more than collecting peaks. It's about standing on sea cliffs watching Atlantic storms approach, climbing mountains that feel genuinely wild, and discovering valleys where sheep outnumber people. It's the kind of walking holiday that reminds you why you fell in love with mountains in the first place.

If you're planning a Donegal walking holiday and want to walk at your own pace without worrying about logistics, we offer self-guided routes across the region with full support—accommodation arranged in handpicked B&Bs, luggage transfers handled, and detailed route notes provided. You carry only essentials and focus on enjoying every step.

Whether this is your first visit to Donegal or you're returning to explore new routes, these trails are calling. The question isn't whether you can do them—it's when you'll start planning your trip. Let's get you to Donegal's peaks and cliffs.
